title: How to Find Mahjong Games Near You: The Complete Guide
description: A practical guide to finding mahjong groups, open play sessions, and lessons in your area — whether you're a beginner, a traveler, or relocating to a new city.
author: MahjNearMe
body:
Mahjong is having a moment. The tile game that was once the domain of grandmothers in community centers is now filling up breweries, bookstores, and Airbnbs. If you're trying to find a game near you — and quickly learned that Google alone doesn't cut it — this guide walks you through every way that actually works.

1. Start with a directory built for mahjong

The first stop should be a directory that specializes in mahjong rather than general event listings. MahjNearMe lists more than 2,000 mahjong games, open-play sessions, classes, and tournaments across all 50 U.S. states. Type your city or ZIP into the search bar, or tap Use my location, and you'll see every nearby group on an interactive map, sorted by distance.

Each listing shows the game type (American, Chinese, or Riichi), day of the week, skill level, and whether the group is drop-in friendly. That last detail matters — not every group welcomes walk-ins, and you'll save everyone time by filtering for it.

2. Check your city page

If you already know where you're searching, skip straight to your city page. We keep curated lists for hundreds of U.S. cities, each with a breakdown of:

  • How many active groups are in the area
  • Whether they're clustered in a particular neighborhood or spread out
  • Beginner-friendly vs. advanced-only groups
  • Drop-in schedules and RSVP requirements

City pages are the fastest path if you're planning a trip, moving somewhere new, or just want to see the scene at a glance.

3. Look up an instructor

If you're a total beginner, jumping into open play can feel intimidating. Instead, find a local instructor. They typically run small classes — four to eight students — that move at a sustainable pace and introduce you to the local community.

Our instructors directory lists teachers by city with their game style, class format, and whether they offer private lessons. Most instructors charge \$20–\$50 per class and will introduce students to drop-in groups once they've got the basics down.

4. Don't forget the obvious places

Before mahjong had dedicated directories, players found games the analog way. These still work:

  • Senior centers and JCCs. Nearly every Jewish Community Center in the country hosts a weekly American Mahjong session. Many are open to non-members.
  • Libraries. Public libraries increasingly host game nights, and mahjong is a staple.
  • Breweries and cafes. Younger players have pushed mahjong into third spaces — search "mahjong night" plus your city on Instagram or TikTok.
  • Meetup and Facebook Groups. Less curated, sometimes stale, but occasionally productive. Search your city name plus "mahjong."

5. Can't find a game? Start one.

If you live somewhere with no listed groups, you are almost certainly not the only mahjong player in town. You're just the first one to look. A few low-friction ways to start:

  1. Post in a local Facebook group or Nextdoor. One or two interested neighbors is enough for a casual weekly game.
  2. Ask a coffee shop, bookstore, or brewery if they'd host a monthly mahjong night. Most will say yes — it fills a slow evening.
  3. List your group on MahjNearMe once you have it going. It's free, and players in your area will find you within a week.

6. Traveling? Plan ahead.

One of the best-kept secrets in mahjong is that many American groups are unexpectedly welcoming to visiting players. If you're on a business trip, vacation, or visiting family, email or DM the organizer a few days in advance. Most will say yes, especially if you mention your home group.

What about online?

In-person beats online for most players — the social element is the whole point. But if you're stuck, platforms like Real Mah Jongg (American), Mahjong Soul (Riichi), and PlayOK (Chinese) all have active communities. Use them as a supplement, not a replacement.

The short version

Use a dedicated directory first. Check your city page. Reach out to an instructor if you're new. Then try the local third places — JCCs, libraries, breweries. If nothing works, start a game yourself; the demand is always higher than the supply.

Ready to find your next game? Search MahjNearMe for groups in your area.
